q: What are the three architectural layers of Snowflake and what does each layer do?
a: Snowflake is built on a multi-cluster shared data architecture composed of three independently scalable layers. The Database Storage layer persists all data in Snowflake-managed cloud object storage (S3, Azure Blob, or GCS) as compressed, columnar micro-partitions; customers never interact with this layer directly and pay only for the volume stored. The Query Processing layer consists of virtual warehouses — MPP compute clusters that execute queries, DML, and data loading entirely independently of storage, so multiple warehouses can read the same data simultaneously without contention. The Cloud Services layer is the coordinating brain: it handles authentication, access control, metadata management, query parsing and optimization, and transaction management; it runs continuously and is billed only when its compute consumption exceeds 10% of the daily warehouse credit usage. This separation means storage and compute can scale independently, and organizations pay only for the compute they actually use.
fj: Snowflake has two layers — a storage layer for holding data and a compute layer for querying it, and both must be provisioned together.
fs: Snowflake has three distinct layers, not two. The Cloud Services layer is a critical third layer that handles all metadata, security, and query optimization. Bundling storage and compute together describes a traditional shared-disk or shared-nothing architecture — exactly what Snowflake's decoupled design avoids.

q: Why can two teams run heavy queries at the same time in Snowflake without one slowing down the other, even though they access the same tables?
a: This is possible because of Snowflake's multi-cluster shared data architecture. Storage is a single shared layer (cloud object storage with micro-partitioned columnar files) that is readable by any warehouse simultaneously without locking. Each team provisions its own virtual warehouse — an independent MPP compute cluster — so their queries execute on separate compute resources. Because warehouses do not share CPU or memory, a large analytics scan by one team does not queue behind or starve a dashboard query from another team. Each warehouse also maintains its own local SSD result cache, so repeated queries within a warehouse are served from cache without hitting storage at all. This workload isolation is a foundational selling point of the Snowflake AI Data Cloud and eliminates the resource contention that plagues traditional shared-disk warehouses.
fj: Snowflake serializes concurrent queries across a shared compute pool and uses a priority queue to prevent resource starvation.
fs: Snowflake does not use a shared compute pool with a priority queue. Each virtual warehouse is an isolated compute cluster. True workload isolation means there is no queue to contend with — teams simply run separate warehouses, each billed independently for actual usage.

code.label: Separate warehouses for separate teams
lines: -- BI dashboard warehouse (runs simultaneously, no contention)
-- Both can query the same table at the same time
SELECT region, SUM(revenue) FROM prod.sales.orders GROUP BY 1;

q: What is a Snowflake Notebook, and how does it handle SQL and Python execution differently within the same session?
a: A Snowflake Notebook is an interactive, cell-based development environment inside Snowsight that lets you mix SQL, Python (via Snowpark), and Markdown cells in a single document. Each user who opens the same notebook gets their own independent session, so session variables and DataFrame state do not bleed across users. SQL cells execute against the notebook's assigned Query Warehouse, which is a standard virtual warehouse that must be running when a SQL cell is executed. Python cells run on the notebook's compute (a separate Snowpark-enabled container), and you access the session object directly via the pre-initialized `session` variable — there is no need to create a Snowpark session manually. Streamlit widgets and charts can be embedded in Python cells using `import streamlit as st`, rendering interactive visualizations inline. When you close and reopen a notebook, it reconnects to the same session if it is still alive, preserving Python variables; however, Streamlit widget state is not persisted across reconnects.
fj: Every user who opens the same notebook shares a single session, so variables set by one user are visible to all others.
fs: Each user gets an isolated, independent session. Shared sessions would cause state collisions and security issues. Snowflake deliberately provides per-user session isolation to ensure that one user's DataFrame or SET variable cannot affect another's work in the same notebook.

code.label: Notebook Python cell — Snowpark + Streamlit
lines: # session is pre-injected — no need to create it

q: Describe Snowflake's object hierarchy from the top-level container down to a column, and name the three types of tables available.
a: Snowflake's object hierarchy is: Organization → Account → Database → Schema → Table/View/Stage/Function (and other schema-level objects) → Column. An Account is the primary unit of isolation and billing; it lives inside an Organization that can contain multiple accounts for governance or geographic separation. A Database is a top-level namespace within an account that groups related schemas. A Schema is a logical container inside a database that groups tables, views, stages, pipes, and other objects; every database automatically gets two system schemas — INFORMATION_SCHEMA and PUBLIC. Tables are the primary structured storage objects inside a schema. Snowflake supports three main persistent table types: Permanent tables (default, with Time Travel up to 90 days and Fail-safe), Transient tables (no Fail-safe, Time Travel limited to 1 day, lower storage cost), and Temporary tables (session-scoped, auto-dropped when the session ends, no Fail-safe). External and Iceberg table types also exist for data that lives outside Snowflake-managed storage.
fj: The hierarchy is Account → Database → Table → Schema, and all tables support 90-day Time Travel by default.
fs: The correct order places Schema between Database and Table (Account → Database → Schema → Table). The hierarchy matters for privilege grants, which follow this containment chain. Also, only Permanent tables support up to 90-day Time Travel; Transient tables max out at 1 day, and Temporary tables are 0 or 1 day depending on configuration.

code.label: Object hierarchy in SQL
lines: -- Organization and account are implicit context
-- Transient: no Fail-safe, cheaper for staging
CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE session_scratch LIKE analytics.sales.orders;

q: What semi-structured data types does Snowflake natively support, and how does the VARIANT type differ from OBJECT and ARRAY?
a: Snowflake provides three native semi-structured data types: VARIANT, OBJECT, and ARRAY. VARIANT is the universal container type — it can hold any value including scalars, JSON objects, arrays, or nested structures of arbitrary depth; it is the most commonly used type when loading JSON, Avro, ORC, or Parquet data because the entire document can be stored as a single VARIANT column. OBJECT is a semi-structured type that specifically represents a key-value map (analogous to a JSON object or Python dict), where keys are always strings and values are VARIANT. ARRAY is a semi-structured type that represents an ordered list of VARIANT values (analogous to a JSON array). Internally, Snowflake stores all three in a compact binary serialization and indexes them in micro-partition metadata to enable efficient columnar access. You can traverse nested structures using colon (:) notation for object key access and bracket ([index]) notation for array element access directly in SQL, enabling SQL-native querying without any need to flatten first.
fj: VARIANT can only store strings and numbers; to store nested JSON you must use OBJECT, which supports arbitrary nesting.
fs: VARIANT is the flexible container that can hold any value including nested objects and arrays — it is not limited to scalars. OBJECT is specifically a key-value map and is itself a specialized form of VARIANT. Loading a whole JSON document into a VARIANT column is the idiomatic Snowflake pattern.

code.label: Querying VARIANT semi-structured data
lines: -- Load raw JSON into a VARIANT column
-- Colon notation for object key access

q: How do you grant and revoke object-level privileges in Snowflake, and what does the principle of least privilege look like in practice?
a: Snowflake uses Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) where privileges are granted to roles, and roles are granted to users or other roles, forming a hierarchy. The GRANT PRIVILEGE command gives a role the ability to perform specific operations on an object (e.g., SELECT on a table, USAGE on a schema). The REVOKE PRIVILEGE command removes those rights. The principle of least privilege means assigning only the minimum permissions necessary: for a read-only analyst, you grant USAGE on the database and schema, then SELECT on specific tables — never granting OWNERSHIP or MODIFY. Sensitive administrative roles like ACCOUNTADMIN should be used sparingly and never for day-to-day data access. A practical pattern is to create purpose-built functional roles (e.g., `analyst_role`, `loader_role`) that own or access only the objects they need, then grant those functional roles to user-facing roles. Privilege grants are visible via SHOW GRANTS ON OBJECT and can be audited through the ACCOUNT_USAGE.GRANTS_TO_ROLES view.
fj: To follow least privilege, just grant users the SYSADMIN role because it can create objects without having full ACCOUNTADMIN permissions.
fs: SYSADMIN is a powerful system role meant for creating and managing databases and warehouses — it is far broader than least privilege for an analyst or service account. Least privilege means creating narrow functional roles with only the exact privileges needed, never defaulting to a system role.

code.label: Least-privilege role setup
lines: GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE analytics          TO ROLE analyst_ro;
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA   analytics.sales    TO ROLE analyst_ro;
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics.sales TO ROLE analyst_ro;
-- Future grants so new tables are auto-accessible
GRANT SELECT ON FUTURE TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics.sales TO ROLE analyst_ro;
GRANT ROLE analyst_ro TO USER alice;

q: Walk through the steps to create a Snowflake database, explore its objects, set a parameter, and transfer ownership to a different role.
a: First, create the database with CREATE DATABASE, optionally specifying DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS for Time Travel. Once created, you can explore its contents in Snowsight under Data → Databases or via SHOW SCHEMAS, SHOW TABLES, and DESCRIBE TABLE commands in SQL. To set a parameter, use ALTER DATABASE ... SET <parameter> = <value> — for example setting DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS = 7 overrides the account default for that database. Ownership transfer uses GRANT OWNERSHIP: GRANT OWNERSHIP ON DATABASE mydb TO ROLE new_owner_role REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS (or COPY CURRENT GRANTS). If you use REVOKE CURRENT GRANTS, all existing outbound privileges on child objects are revoked and must be re-granted by the new owner; COPY CURRENT GRANTS preserves them. The role executing the transfer must currently own the database or be ACCOUNTADMIN. After transfer, the old owner loses all privileges unless they were explicitly re-granted.
fj: You can transfer ownership by simply running ALTER DATABASE mydb SET OWNER = new_role.
fs: There is no SET OWNER clause on ALTER DATABASE. Ownership transfer in Snowflake is always done with the GRANT OWNERSHIP statement, which requires choosing how to handle existing outbound privileges (REVOKE or COPY CURRENT GRANTS). Using the wrong syntax will produce a parse error.

code.label: Create, configure, and transfer a database
lines: -- Step 4: Transfer ownership, preserving existing grants

q: What SQL commands are used to create, alter, and drop a schema in Snowflake, and how does ownership of the schema affect child object access?
a: CREATE SCHEMA <db>.<schema_name> creates a new schema; adding OR REPLACE drops and recreates it atomically, while IF NOT EXISTS makes the statement a no-op when the schema already exists. You can set schema-level parameters at creation or later with ALTER SCHEMA ... SET, including DATA_RETENTION_TIME_IN_DAYS and DEFAULT_DDL_COLLATION. DROP SCHEMA removes the schema and all its objects; adding IF EXISTS prevents an error if it does not exist, and adding CASCADE explicitly drops all child objects (the default behavior). Ownership of a schema grants the owning role full control over the schema itself — CREATE TABLE, ALTER SCHEMA, DROP SCHEMA — but does NOT automatically grant SELECT on tables inside the schema to other roles. Those table-level privileges must be granted separately. This distinction matters because transferring schema ownership with GRANT OWNERSHIP does not propagate SELECT rights on existing tables to the new owner's grantees unless COPY CURRENT GRANTS is used and the existing grants include those table privileges.
fj: The role that owns a schema automatically has SELECT on all tables within it, so no separate GRANT SELECT is needed.
fs: Ownership of a schema grants DDL control over the schema container, not DML access to data inside it. A role must be explicitly granted SELECT on tables within the schema, or be granted a role that has SELECT. This is a common privilege misconfiguration in Snowflake access setups.

code.label: Schema lifecycle operations
lines: CREATE SCHEMA IF NOT EXISTS analytics.staging
-- Drop schema and all objects inside it

q: What is a Snowflake directory table, and how do you use GET_PRESIGNED_URL to share an unstructured file without requiring Snowflake credentials?
a: A directory table is a special metadata table attached to an internal or external stage that automatically catalogs every file on the stage with columns including RELATIVE_PATH, SIZE, LAST_MODIFIED, MD5, ETAG, and FILE_URL. You enable it at stage creation with DIRECTORY = (ENABLE = TRUE) or add it later with ALTER STAGE. The directory table is refreshed manually with ALTER STAGE ... REFRESH or automatically on external stages via a cloud event notification. To provide temporary access to a file without requiring Snowflake credentials, use the GET_PRESIGNED_URL function: it accepts the stage name and relative file path and returns a time-limited HTTPS URL that any HTTP client can fetch without authentication. The expiration window is configurable (default 3600 seconds). Note that GET_PRESIGNED_URL does not work on client-side encrypted files — the stage must use server-side encryption (SNOWFLAKE_SSE) for the URL to be readable.
fj: Directory tables only work with external stages on S3; you must use GET_FILE_URL (not GET_PRESIGNED_URL) to generate shareable links.
fs: Directory tables work on both internal and external stages. GET_PRESIGNED_URL is specifically designed for generating shareable, credential-free URLs, while GET_FILE_URL returns a Snowflake-scoped URL that still requires valid Snowflake authentication to access. Using the wrong function will expose a URL that only authenticated Snowflake users can open, defeating the sharing purpose.

code.label: Directory table + pre-signed URL
lines: -- Create stage with directory table enabled
-- Refresh to pick up newly uploaded files
-- Generate a pre-signed URL valid for 1 hour

q: How do you configure auto-suspend and auto-resume on a Snowflake virtual warehouse, and what are the recommended settings for different workload types?
a: AUTO_SUSPEND specifies the number of seconds of inactivity after which a warehouse is automatically suspended and stops consuming credits; AUTO_RESUME = TRUE means the warehouse starts automatically when any SQL statement is submitted to it. Both are set at warehouse creation with CREATE WAREHOUSE or changed at any time with ALTER WAREHOUSE. The minimum AUTO_SUSPEND value is 60 seconds, and setting it to 0 or NULL disables automatic suspension — this is not recommended unless a continuously-warm cache is critical for latency-sensitive workloads. For ad-hoc and data science workloads where cache reuse is unlikely, Snowflake recommends AUTO_SUSPEND = 300 (5 minutes) to avoid unnecessary credit burn. For BI and dashboard warehouses where result cache reuse is important, AUTO_SUSPEND = 600 (10 minutes) or longer is recommended to keep the cache warm across user sessions. The suspend check runs approximately every 30 seconds, so the actual suspension time may be up to 30 seconds after the configured threshold. AUTO_RESUME = FALSE is valid but means the warehouse must be started manually, which is appropriate for scheduled batch jobs where surprise auto-start would cause unintended charges.
fj: Setting AUTO_SUSPEND = 1 saves the most credits because the warehouse suspends almost immediately after each query.
fs: Setting AUTO_SUSPEND to the minimum (60 seconds, not 1) can actually increase costs for BI workloads because every new query incurs a cold-start and cache-miss penalty. Cache warmup and the 60-second-minimum billing interval mean that extremely short suspend windows waste startup overhead. The recommended values balance idle cost against cache reuse for each workload type.

code.label: Auto-suspend / auto-resume by workload type
lines: -- BI dashboard: keep cache warm for users
-- Ad-hoc / data science: cache not reused
-- Batch ETL: manual resume, no auto-start surprises

q: What is Snowflake Fail-safe, how long does it last, and how does it differ from Time Travel?
a: Fail-safe is a non-configurable, 7-day data protection period that begins immediately after a table's Time Travel retention period ends. During Fail-safe, data is retained by Snowflake's internal systems as a last-resort disaster recovery mechanism for catastrophic failures such as hardware faults or internal system errors. Unlike Time Travel, Fail-safe is not user-accessible via SQL — you cannot query historical data in Fail-safe yourself or restore it with AT/BEFORE syntax. Recovery requires engaging Snowflake Support, and the process may take several hours to several days with no SLA guarantee. Fail-safe applies only to Permanent tables; Transient and Temporary tables have no Fail-safe period to reduce storage costs. The combined data lifecycle for a Permanent table is: active data → Time Travel (0–90 days, configurable) → Fail-safe (fixed 7 days) → purged. Data storage consumed by both Time Travel and Fail-safe is billed at the standard Snowflake storage rate.
fj: Fail-safe gives you a 7-day window after deletion where you can use the AT clause in SELECT queries to retrieve the data yourself.
fs: The AT/BEFORE clause is part of Time Travel, not Fail-safe. Fail-safe data is inaccessible to users via SQL and can only be recovered by Snowflake Support responding to a filed case. Confusing the two leads to false confidence — if Time Travel has expired, the data is not self-serviceable.

q: How does Snowflake protect data at rest and in transit, and what is the hierarchical key model used for at-rest encryption?
a: All data at rest in Snowflake is encrypted using AES-256, applied automatically to every micro-partition file stored in cloud object storage. Snowflake uses a hierarchical key model with four tiers: the root key (Account Master Key) is stored in a cloud-provider Hardware Security Module (HSM); database and stage-level keys are derived from the root key; file-level keys encrypt individual micro-partition files; and data encryption keys (DEKs) encrypt individual data blocks. Snowflake automatically rotates keys that are older than 30 days and can re-encrypt (rekey) all data files when needed — both operations happen transparently with no user action required. For data in transit, Snowflake enforces TLS 1.2 or higher for all connections between clients, drivers, and the Snowflake service. For customers with stricter compliance requirements, Snowflake offers Tri-Secret Secure (Business Critical edition), which adds a customer-managed key to the hierarchy so that Snowflake cannot decrypt data without the customer's participation.
fj: Snowflake uses AES-128 for at-rest encryption and relies on the cloud provider's default TLS version, which may be TLS 1.0.
fs: Snowflake uses AES-256 (not AES-128) for at-rest encryption, which is the stronger industry standard. For in-transit protection, Snowflake requires TLS 1.2 or higher — TLS 1.0 is explicitly not supported. These are commonly tested facts on the SnowPro Associate exam.
